lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced cargo transportation on rivers, lakes, and intracoastal waterways outside the Great Lakes System, including crewed barge and towing operations that move bulk liquids, grain, coal, aggregates, chemicals, petroleum products, and other freight.
lens.excluded_activities: Coastal and Great Lakes freight, deep-sea freight, harbor tug services, passenger transport, captive shipper fleets, fleeting or terminal-only services classified elsewhere, dormant vessel-owning shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without transferable operating organizations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industrial, agricultural, energy, construction, and trading customers buy contracts of affreightment, voyage movements, or time-charter capacity under annual or multi-year term contracts and spot agreements; operators provide towboats, barges, qualified crews, dispatch, maintenance, regulatory compliance, and accountable delivery through the inland waterway network.

a.rationale: The broad water-transportation occupation mix assigns 18.84% of employment to office and administrative support, with additional business, finance, management, dispatch, freight-agent, billing, bookkeeping, and clerk roles. AI can assist quoting, tow and crew planning, customer updates, document intake, billing, compliance drafting, maintenance records, and exception triage. Wage weighting and existing dispatch systems limit incremental exposure, while licensed wheelhouse work, engineering, deck operations, cargo handling, maintenance, lock transits, and emergency response remain physical and safety-critical.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study at several dry- and liquid-bulk LMM operators mapping current payroll to sales, dispatch, voyage planning, documentation, billing, compliance, maintenance, vessel operations, and already-automated tasks would materially change the range.
caveats: BLS reports NAICS 483000 rather than 483211 and therefore mixes inland, coastal, passenger, and freight operators.
Employment shares are not wage shares or task shares, and no source directly measures current AI exposure in inland barge firms.
The estimate excludes already-automated work and does not assume AI removes required crew positions.

rho.rationale: A majority of the exposed shore-side opportunity could be implemented within five years through human-approved copilots and workflow integration. Fragmented dispatch, maintenance, accounting, customer, and vessel systems; variable river conditions; weak connectivity; cybersecurity; auditability; union and crewing practices; and safety-management obligations constrain adoption. GAO's finding that commercial autonomy remains narrow supports treating onboard AI as decision support rather than crew replacement.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after operating data from several LMM inland carriers covering manual touches, errors, overrides, dispatch performance, demurrage, safety findings, cyber controls, employee adoption, and realized avoided hires through high- and low-water seasons would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No source measures implementation of generative AI in the LMM inland-freight population.
Pilots during normal river conditions may not generalize to closures, extreme water levels, cargo incidents, or lock delays.
The estimate applies only to the opportunity counted in a, not to total labor.

e.bridge: Kirby's filing describes the US inland tank-barge segment, not all dry and liquid cargo firms in the modeled LMM count, as a mixture of large integrated companies, small operators, and captive refinery or petrochemical fleets. I use that ownership mix and the official service definition to discount n for captives, vessel shells, single-project or terminal-only businesses, distressed or noncompliant fleets, and firms lacking transferable crews, customer relationships, or management. Tank-barge evidence may overstate captive prevalence relative to dry bulk, biasing eligibility downward.
e.rationale: The official industry definition is centered on external cargo transport, so a majority of active private LMM firms should fit the recurring-service lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive fleets, asset-only entities, extreme customer concentration, deficient safety records, unsustainable maintenance needs, and operating authority or customer vetting that cannot survive a change of control.
e.change_evidence: A named-firm census of the 55 modeled LMM firms with revenue source, cargo and route mix, captive status, vessel ownership, certificates of inspection, safety record, customer recurrence and concentration, crew depth, and transferable management would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The supplied LMM firm count is estimated from receipts and a margin bridge rather than observed firm EBITDA.
Kirby's market description covers inland tank barges and may not represent dry-cargo towing firms.
NAICS is assigned at establishment level while eligibility is a firm-level control-transfer concept.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age data and the Exit Planning Institute survey cover employer-business owners across US industries, not inland freight operators or completed control transfers. Census reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were at least 55, and 49% of the exit-planning survey respondents wanted to exit within five years. I discount those signals for internal succession, delayed plans, closures, failed sales, asset-only dispositions, environmental and safety liabilities, and financing complexity; the self-selected planning survey likely overstates realized qualifying transfers.
s5.rationale: Broad owner demographics indicate succession pressure among private operators, but a regulated, capital-intensive carrier can take substantial time to diligence and finance. Vessel condition, customer vetting, environmental exposure, crew retention, and concentrated shipper relationships can turn intended exits into delayed or nonqualifying outcomes.
s5.change_evidence: An inland-marine owner-age and succession survey plus five years of completed control transfers identified through Coast Guard vessel-documentation changes, PitchBook, GF Data, public merger filings, and specialist maritime brokers would materially change the range.
caveats: Neither source isolates transportation, maritime firms, the LMM EBITDA band, or completed control transfers.
The Census owner-age statistic uses 2018 data and responding employer businesses only.
Exit intent can produce a closure, family transfer, or asset sale that does not qualify.

q.bridge: Kirby is a large public tank-barge operator rather than an LMM cross-industry sample, and its contract disclosures measure pricing structure rather than retained AI benefit. In 2025, 70% of inland marine revenue was under term contracts and 30% under spot contracts; most contracts by revenue were for one year, while some multi-year contracts had labor and producer-price escalators. I translate this into meaningful early retention followed by renewal and market leakage. Large scale and customer relationships may give Kirby stronger retention than LMM firms, biasing the proxy upward.
q.rationale: Term contracts and time charters sell transport capacity and accountable execution rather than administrative hours, allowing some workflow savings to remain with the carrier initially. Annual renewals, market-priced spot work, shipper procurement, and explicit cost escalators create mechanisms for customers and competition to reclaim savings. Five-year discounted retention should therefore be material but far below full benefit.
q.change_evidence: Redacted LMM contracts, bid sheets, rate formulas, open-book provisions, labor and fuel escalators, renewal histories, spot-versus-term mix, and gross-margin retention following documented workflow savings would materially change the estimate.
caveats: One large tank-barge operator may not represent smaller dry-cargo, grain, aggregate, or specialty operators.
The filing describes cost escalation and market pricing, not AI-savings pass-through.
Retention varies with route scarcity, fleet availability, cargo specialization, and customer concentration.

d5.bridge: USACE measures current inland-navigation tonnage and commodity mix, USDA measures recent barged-grain movements, and BLS projects water-transportation worker employment; none directly measures year-five constant-price, constant-quality demand for the 483211 LMM service basket. I center the range near flat using BLS's 1% ten-year employment growth, temper that with 2025 grain volume 11% above 2024, and widen it for the USACE-reported exposure to coal, petroleum, aggregates, chemicals, and grain. Employment may understate quantity if productivity rises; one strong grain year may overstate durable demand.
d5.rationale: Inland barging remains a low-cost link for bulk commodities, and 2025 grain movements were robust despite high water, low water, ice, draft limits, and tow-size restrictions. Demand is nevertheless cyclical and heterogeneous: grain exports and petrochemicals can grow while coal declines, pipelines displace some liquids, and extreme river conditions constrain usable capacity. The five-year real service quantity is therefore likely close to current levels with meaningful two-sided risk.
d5.change_evidence: FAF and USACE forecasts for 2026-to-2031 inland tonnage by commodity and waterway, customer production and export forecasts, contracted barge-days, lock throughput, empty-barge ratios, and real rate and utilization data for a representative operator panel would materially change the range.
caveats: The USACE fast facts mix project tonnage and broader system statements and do not provide a five-year forecast.
USDA grain is only one commodity family and 2025 growth may reverse with crops, exports, or river conditions.
Employment is an imperfect demand proxy because productivity and minimum crewing can change independently of tonnage.

o.bridge: Coast Guard towing-vessel guidance and GAO's autonomy review apply broadly to US commercial vessels rather than measuring the year-five operator-required quantity share in NAICS 483211. The Coast Guard assigns owners, managing operators, and masters responsibility for safe manning and requires certificate-of-inspection crew complements and credentials; GAO finds current commercial autonomous uses narrow and statutory crew reductions constrained. I translate these facts into a high operator-required share. The proxy could overstate durability if remote operations or crew-law changes progress unexpectedly fast.
o.rationale: Cargo may move to rail, truck, or pipeline, which belongs in d5, but freight that remains on inland waterways still requires an accountable carrier to control compliant vessels, qualified crews, navigation, maintenance, cargo safety, environmental response, customer performance, and lock and river execution. AI can reduce administrative work and augment decisions without eliminating that operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Coast Guard approvals for materially reduced-crew autonomous towing operations, statutory amendments, insurer and classification acceptance, sustained safety results, and shipper adoption of remote-only or self-operated barge services would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The evidence establishes current legal and operational accountability rather than a measured future quantity share.
Remote operations could relocate work while retaining an accountable carrier.
Modal substitution and commodity-volume loss are assigned to d5 rather than counted again here.

narrative.executive_view: Inland water freight is labor-intensive but only a minority of work is exposed to AI because vessel operation, deck work, engineering, maintenance, and cargo safety dominate. The implementable opportunity lies in quoting, dispatch support, customer updates, documentation, billing, compliance preparation, and maintenance planning. Regulated towing operations remain operator-dependent, while term contracts offer partial but temporary insulation from benefit pass-through.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can structure shipping orders, assist tow and crew schedules, summarize river and maintenance information, draft customer and compliance communications, reconcile voyage bills, and flag anomalies. Human dispatchers must handle river closures and exceptions, and credentialed crews remain responsible for navigation, watchkeeping, cargo, and emergencies. Full vessel autonomy is not a credible five-year labor case.
narrative.value_capture: Term charters and contracts of affreightment can preserve early savings because pricing buys transport capacity and service outcomes. Yet annual renewals, competitive spot markets, and explicit labor, inflation, and fuel escalators expose costs and margins to customers. Operators that use savings to improve vessel utilization, reliability, and avoided hiring should retain more than operators competing on transparent commodity routes.
narrative.firm_availability: The 55-firm LMM count is modeled and should not be treated as a verified target roster. The inland industry includes integrated carriers, small operators, and captive fleets; eligibility depends on finding a standalone external-service company with compliant vessels, durable customers, crews, and management. Broad succession indicators imply supply, but completed inland-marine control-transfer data are missing.
narrative.demand_durability: Inland waterways move large volumes of grain, coal, petroleum, aggregates, chemicals, and other bulk freight at cost advantages to rail. Recent grain volume is strong, but coal decline, pipeline competition, commodity cycles, export policy, lock reliability, drought, flood, and ice create large route-specific swings. Overall five-year real quantity appears close to stable, and remaining waterborne quantity still needs an accountable operator.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main evidence gaps are a 483211-specific task study, an observed LMM target list, closed-deal data, and representative LMM contract samples. Vessel age, dry-dock capital, safety or pollution events, customer concentration, crew shortages, cyber failures, locks, and extreme river levels can overwhelm administrative savings. A weak fleet or commodity route can be unattractive even if AI workflows succeed.
narrative.principal_driver: A high labor base and repeat term-contract workflows create usable shore-side efficiency while regulation preserves the carrier's operating role.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor is physical or safety-critical, and demand varies materially with commodities, waterways, weather, and infrastructure reliability.

sources.title: NAICS Sector 48 Definitions: 483211 Inland Water Freight Transportation
sources.supports: Defines 483211 as cargo transportation on lakes, rivers, or intracoastal waterways outside the Great Lakes System and distinguishes coastal and deep-sea freight.

sources.title: Water Transportation - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Reports the NAICS 483 occupation mix, including 18.84% office and administrative support employment and detailed billing, bookkeeping, customer-service, freight-agent, dispatcher, and clerk roles.

sources.title: Kirby Corporation 2025 Annual Report
sources.supports: Describes inland tank-barge ownership structure, contract and spot mix, term lengths, time charters, labor and fuel escalators, utilization, commodity demand drivers, vessel regulation, and customer relationships.

sources.title: USACE Inland Navigation 2022 National Report: Value to the Nation Fast Facts
sources.supports: Reports inland-navigation tonnage by commodity, 397.2 million tons of estimated project tonnage, over 500 million system tons annually, and the system's cost advantage versus rail.

sources.title: USDA Grain Transportation Report: Review of 2025 Barged Grain Movements and Rates
sources.supports: Reports 2025 Mississippi River System grain-barge volume 11% above 2024, strong corn exports, divergent soybean and wheat movements, and disruptions from water, ice, draft, and tow-size restrictions.

sources.title: Coast Guard: Autonomous Ships and Efforts to Regulate Them
sources.supports: Finds current commercial autonomous-ship uses are narrow and generally retain human control, with safety, cyber, legal, and statutory minimum-crewing constraints in US waters.

sources.title: US Coast Guard: Towing Vessel Credentials, Documents and Records
sources.supports: States owner, managing-operator, and master responsibilities for safe manning and watchkeeping and requires the credentialed crew complement specified by a towing vessel's certificate of inspection.

sources.title: 2023 National State of Owner Readiness Report
sources.supports: Reports that 49% of 1,162 surveyed US business owners wanted to exit within five years and that 70% preferred an internal transition, cross-industry intent evidence rather than completed transfers.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey using 2018 data.

sources.title: Water Transportation Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Projects water-transportation-worker employment to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034 and describes vessel-operation, maintenance, credentialing, and replacement-opening needs.
